name: spawn-agent
description: Spawn an AI coding agent in a new terminal (Claude, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, Copilot). Defaults to Claude Code if unspecified.
capabilities: Launch Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, or Copilot in new terminals
Capture and retrieve agent output for later review
Configure model selection, permission modes, and interactive settings per agent
Handle authentication flows for CLI tools that require login
author: Consiliency
securitySummary: Static scanner flagged 447 patterns, all evaluated as false positives. The skill is a legitimate developer tool that spawns AI coding agents in terminal windows. All subprocess calls use hardcoded command strings (not user input), temp file paths are controlled and predictable, and there is no credential exfiltration or malicious behavior. The scanner misinterpreted markdown documentation examples as code execution and flagged normal terminal operations as suspicious patterns.
valueStatement: Managing multiple AI coding agents requires switching between different CLI tools. This skill launches Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, or Copilot in a fresh terminal window. Developers can offload complex coding tasks to specialized agents while continuing their main workflow.
userTitle: Spawn AI coding agents in new terminals
limitations: Requires CLI tools to be installed and configured on the system
Output capture requires understanding each agent's JSON output flags
Interactive mode behavior varies between different AI CLI tools

useCases.title: Offload complex refactoring
useCases.description: Spawn a heavy Opus-model agent in a new terminal to handle large-scale code refactoring while you continue code review.

useCases.title: Parallel backend and frontend work
useCases.description: Run Gemini CLI for backend API work in one terminal while using Claude Code for frontend debugging in another.

useCases.title: Infrastructure automation
useCases.description: Launch Cursor agent with workspace permissions to automate Terraform configurations and deployment scripts.

promptTemplates.title: Basic agent spawn
promptTemplates.prompt: Spawn an agent to analyze the codebase structure and identify the main components.

promptTemplates.title: Specific model request
promptTemplates.scenario: Request heavy model for complex task
promptTemplates.prompt: Spawn agent use claude opus to review all authentication code paths and identify security issues.

promptTemplates.title: Context transfer
promptTemplates.prompt: Spawn agent use claude code to continue the work we discussed. Summarize work so far: implemented user auth module, need to add password reset flow.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-agent coordination
promptTemplates.scenario: Use different providers for parallel work
promptTemplates.prompt: Spawn two agents in parallel: one using codex to write unit tests, another using gemini to generate API documentation.

bestPractices: Run the CLI --help command first to verify available options before spawning agents
Use model selection flags to match task complexity (fast models for simple tasks, heavy models for complex analysis)
Enable LOG_AGENT_OUTPUT to capture clean JSON responses for later processing
antiPatterns: Spawning agents without reading the cookbook for that specific CLI tool
Skipping the --help verification step assuming syntax is correct
Using interactive mode flags incorrectly which causes agents to exit immediately after task completion

faq.question: Which AI agents are supported?
faq.answer: Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Google Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, and GitHub Copilot CLI tools are supported.

faq.question: How does agent selection work?
faq.answer: Explicit requests use the specified agent. Without specification, DEFAULT_AGENT (claude-code) is used. Check ENABLED flags for availability.

faq.question: Can I capture agent output?
faq.answer: Yes, set LOG_AGENT_OUTPUT true (default) to write clean JSON to temp files. Use read_fork_output() to retrieve results.

faq.question: Do I need to install CLI tools?
faq.answer: Yes, each agent CLI must be installed and authenticated on your system before this skill can spawn them.

faq.question: What terminal environments work?
faq.answer: Supports macOS Terminal.app, Windows Terminal, WSL, tmux, and fallback background processes on Linux.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication?
faq.answer: Use fork_for_auth(provider) to spawn login terminals. Authenticate in the spawned terminal, then close it to continue.
